title: Authentication Type Distribution
dataSources: Domain controller security, IdP logs with `signature` or `app` denoting mechanism
spl:
index=security tag=authentication
| stats count by signature, app
| sort -count
description: Tracks Kerberos vs. NTLM vs. SAML mix to spot downgrade attacks and legacy protocol use.
value: Tracks Kerberos vs. NTLM vs. SAML mix to spot downgrade attacks and legacy protocol use.
implementation: Baseline per business unit; alert on new `signature` values or surges in `NTLM` where Kerberos is expected.
visualization: Pie chart (mechanism), Bar chart (over time via `timechart`).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.signature, Authentication.app
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: AD, IdP TAs → CIM Authentication.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Domain controller security, IdP logs with `signature` or `app` denoting mechanism.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Baseline per business unit; alert on new `signature` values or surges in `NTLM` where Kerberos is expected.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=security tag=authentication
| stats count by signature, app
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Authentication Type Distribution** — Tracks Kerberos vs. NTLM vs. SAML mix to spot downgrade attacks and legacy protocol use.

Documented **Data sources**: Domain controller security, IdP logs with `signature` or `app` denoting mechanism. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): AD, IdP TAs → CIM Authentication. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: security.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=security, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by signature, app** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.signature, Authentication.app
| sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Authentication Type Distribution** — Tracks Kerberos vs. NTLM vs. SAML mix to spot downgrade attacks and legacy protocol use.

Documented **Data sources**: Domain controller security, IdP logs with `signature` or `app` denoting mechanism. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): AD, IdP TAs → CIM Authentication. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Pie chart (mechanism), Bar chart (over time via `timechart`).
grandmaExplanation: We track Kerberos vs — so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
